1c01496e0668f83b.rule: Stop over-emphasizing 'vampire pricing'; treat it as a pricing strategy, not a newsworthy theme worth foregrounding in content.
1c01496e0668f83b.why: Matt has told the agent many times it overweights this; it's not newsworthy.
1c01496e0668f83b.when: Writing/editing Zerg blog and content copy
1c01496e0668f83b.example_before: headline/section foregrounding vampire pricing as a story
1c01496e0668f83b.example_after: treat vampire pricing as a minor strategy mention, not a theme

1c01496e0668f83b.rule: Don't reference the Securitize SPAC specifically; frame the line as cumulative follow-on funding raised, not Securitize/SPAC.
1c01496e0668f83b.why: Matt does not want SPAC/Securitize called out specifically in public-facing copy.
1c01496e0668f83b.when: Writing public signals / funding lines in Zerg copy
1c01496e0668f83b.example_after: cumulative follow-on funding raised across the cohort

1c01496e0668f83b.rule: Do not list DoorDeck publicly as a client or investment.
1c01496e0668f83b.why: They weren't a great client or investment.
1c01496e0668f83b.when: Listing clients/portfolio in public-facing Zerg copy

1c01496e0668f83b.rule: Open posts with a complete sentence; if using a fragment, commit to it fully rather than half-fragment.
1c01496e0668f83b.why: Half-fragment openers read as weird/unintentional in Matt's voice.
1c01496e0668f83b.when: Drafting blog/announcement intros in Matt's voice.
1c01496e0668f83b.example_before: First sentence not being a proper sentence

1c01496e0668f83b.rule: Avoid self-congratulatory phrasing like 'been a long time coming' or aggrandizing Matt's role/remit.
1c01496e0668f83b.why: Matt finds it cocky, overly formal, and too transparent; he wants understated, less self-aggrandizing copy.
1c01496e0668f83b.example_before: Been a long time coming; describing my remit/role
1c01496e0668f83b.example_after: excited to be working with Idan again

1c01496e0668f83b.rule: Use round, approximate numbers (e.g. 'about 90% less') instead of overly precise figures in prose.
1c01496e0668f83b.why: Matt prefers to keep it simple and move on rather than nail exact multipliers.
1c01496e0668f83b.when: Stating metrics/claims in blog or marketing prose.

1c01496e0668f83b.rule: Drop inline source-citation parentheticals like '(per durable.com/about, 2026)' from case-study prose, and don't split dates awkwardly across two lines.
1c01496e0668f83b.why: Citations read as weird and add little value; split dates look lazy/poorly formatted.
1c01496e0668f83b.when: Writing case studies and prose in Matt's voice.

1c01496e0668f83b.rule: Never name Idan (or other individuals) on Matt's personal website.
1c01496e0668f83b.why: Matt finds naming a colleague on his personal site weird.

1c01496e0668f83b.rule: Avoid "X, not Y" constructions and platitude pivots that lack a concrete mechanism; cut platitudes unless backed by specifics.
1c01496e0668f83b.why: These are AI-writing tells (per Wikipedia Signs of AI Writing) that erode credibility; codified in writing_style.md after Idan's feedback.
1c01496e0668f83b.when: Editing any prose, blog post, thesis piece, or launch copy before publication.

1c01496e0668f83b.rule: Frame content honestly as articles vs research and eliminate AI writing signals to protect credibility with skeptical technical readers.
1c01496e0668f83b.why: Idan's quality-first content strategy emphasizes positioning and credibility over volume; mis-framing damages trust with CTOs.
1c01496e0668f83b.when: Drafting or reviewing thought pieces, case studies, and thesis content for external publication.

1c01496e0668f83b.rule: In case studies, include authentic customer voice and verify all date math, never overselling completion in headline vs body.
1c01496e0668f83b.why: A vendor-self-narrative without customer voice and with date errors damages credibility with skeptical readers (Idan ship-blockers on Durable).
1c01496e0668f83b.when: Writing or reviewing customer case studies and their cover stats/dek.

1c01496e0668f83b.rule: Label every source/reference URL — internal and external alike — with a live or to-build/roadmapped status marker; do not leave internal Zerg paths unmarked.
1c01496e0668f83b.why: Matt's honest-scoping rule applies equally to internal and external links; unmarked internal paths break parity with verified external sources (flagged HIGH in copyedit review).
1c01496e0668f83b.when: Copyediting prose/launch posts that cite source or product URLs

1c01496e0668f83b.rule: Delete AI writing tells: overused words (delve, leverage, multifaceted), filler hedges ('It's important to note that'), and reflexive 'Not X, but Y' contrast frames; apply stricter rules to public prose than email/personal.
1c01496e0668f83b.why: Matt's style philosophy is to kill LLM-generation tells, not enforce formulae; voice rules are context-stratified and context-specific rules win on conflict.
1c01496e0668f83b.when: Copyediting any prose, with strictest application on public Zerg-facing prose

1c01496e0668f83b.rule: Keep the spin-off social/content account voice recognizably similar to Matt's own voice.
1c01496e0668f83b.why: The account is essentially a FakeMatt content channel, so it should sound like him.
1c01496e0668f83b.when: Drafting content/captions for Matt's Instagram/social spin-off account

1c01496e0668f83b.rule: Always add creative flair, cross-over, and humor to Matt's content projects.
1c01496e0668f83b.why: Matt wants his spin-off content to feel creative and playful, not dry.
1c01496e0668f83b.when: Creative content/social projects under Matthew's personal slate

1c01496e0668f83b.rule: Differentiate Matt's personal writing style from Matt-for-Zerg writing style; style changes should barely affect personal use but significantly shape Zerg voice.
1c01496e0668f83b.why: Personal and Zerg professional voices are distinct and should be governed asymmetrically.
1c01496e0668f83b.when: Any prose/voice editing where audience is personal vs Zerg

c5c1679c7ebdb97a.rule: Keep em-dash count to ≤ 3 in pieces of ≤ 800 words; convert conversational asides to periods
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.why: Pulp/social register reads best with periods; em-dashes should be load-bearing, not decorative filler
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.when: Short pulp-register blog posts (≤ 800 words) distributed on social or LinkedIn
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.example_before: …trying again — often with the same broken approach…
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.example_after: …trying again. Often with the same broken approach…

c5c1679c7ebdb97a.rule: Give sharp single-sentence central-frame claims their own paragraph
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.why: White space sells punchlines; burying them mid-paragraph dilutes their impact
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.when: Pulp-register blog posts where one sentence carries the core argument
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.example_before: The retry loop is the default, and the default is wrong. We notice this at Zerg, so a new paper…
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.example_after:
The retry loop is the default, and the default is wrong.

We notice this at Zerg…

c5c1679c7ebdb97a.rule: Cap figure captions at ≤ 20 words in pulp; use numeric shorthand when data supports; cut any caption sentence that restates the body
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.why: Pulp social captions are billboards, not paragraphs; duplication wastes reader attention
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.when: Figures and images in short pulp or social blog posts
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.example_before: Figure 1 shows the dramatic reduction from 1,178 tokens to 174 and then 78, while single-pass correctness improved from 22% to 54%.

c5c1679c7ebdb97a.rule: Name the baseline explicitly whenever citing a speedup or improvement ratio
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.why: Vague baselines undermine credibility with sharp readers and obscure what was actually measured
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.when: Any quantitative claim in blog posts, explainers, or launch copy using 'over baseline' / 'vs baseline' / 'than baseline'

c5c1679c7ebdb97a.rule: Use exactly one call to action in the closer; choose the more confident and specific one
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.why: Two CTAs split focus and dilute the closer's punch; filler CTAs weaken the specific one
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.when: Pulp blog post closers and email sign-offs
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.example_before: we'd love to compare notes — come argue with us about what should and shouldn't transfer.
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.example_after: Come argue with us about what should and shouldn't transfer.

c5c1679c7ebdb97a.rule: Replace 'competitive with X' / 'comparable to X' / 'in line with X' with a verb-forward phrase grounded in the actual data
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.why: Pulp register wants verbs; nominal comparisons are weak and imprecise
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.when: Performance claims or comparisons in blog posts and launch copy

c5c1679c7ebdb97a.rule: Move gap-admission and strategic-disclosure lines to a separate 'Strategic disclosure' section rather than flagging them as writing-quality findings
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.why: These are CEO/author judgment calls, not copyedit issues; mixing them confuses the review and the responsibility
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.when: Copy reviews of blog posts or launch copy that include lines admitting a product gap or unfinished capability
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.example_before: [Flagging 'we don't have ours yet' as a soft writing finding under prose quality]
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.example_after: [Move to 'Strategic disclosure' section with note: author/CEO should decide whether to keep this admission]

c5c1679c7ebdb97a.rule: When a draft title uses a 'What X Gets Wrong About Y' or 'Why X Is Failing Y' curiosity-gap shape, find the flat declarative version of the same claim in the body or share copy and recommend it as the primary title
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.why: Flat declarative headlines are sharper and more pulp; curiosity-gap shapes are overused and signal dated content
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.when: Headline review for pulp-register blog posts, especially those with existing LinkedIn share copy
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.example_before: What AI Agents Get Wrong About Failure
c5c1679c7ebdb97a.example_after: Most AI Agents Have No Memory
